questions.questionText: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (RDS/HMD) is caused by:
questions.optionB: Surfactant deficiency in premature lungs (Type II pneumocytes immature — surfactant production adequate after 34-36 weeks)
questions.explanation: RDS (Hyaline Membrane Disease): surfactant deficiency in premature infants. Surfactant produced by Type II pneumocytes, adequate after 34-36 weeks gestation. Without surfactant → alveolar collapse (atelectasis) → hypoxia → hyaline membranes. CXR: diffuse ground-glass opacity with air bronchograms, low lung volumes. Prevention: antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone/dexamethasone to mother 24-34 weeks — accelerates lung maturity). Treatment: exogenous surfactant replacement (intratracheal), CPAP, mechanical ventilation. L/S ratio ≥2: mature lungs. Reference: OP Ghai — RDS.

questions.questionText: Neonatal jaundice requiring phototherapy is most commonly due to:
questions.optionB: Physiological jaundice (unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia — due to immature hepatic conjugation, increased RBC turnover)
questions.explanation: Physiological jaundice: most common cause of neonatal jaundice. Appears: day 2-3 in term (day 1-2 in preterm). Peaks: day 3-5 (term), day 5-7 (preterm). Resolves: by 2 weeks (term), 3 weeks (preterm). Mechanisms: increased bilirubin production (high RBC mass, short RBC lifespan), immature hepatic UGT1A1 (conjugating enzyme), increased enterohepatic circulation. Pathological jaundice (needs workup): appears <24 hours, rises >5 mg/dL/day, total bilirubin >15 (term) or >12 (preterm), conjugated >2, persists >2 weeks. Treatment: phototherapy (blue light 425-475nm — photoisomerization). Reference: OP Ghai — Neonatal Jaundice.

questions.questionText: APGAR score at 1 and 5 minutes assesses:
questions.optionB: Appearance (color), Pulse (heart rate), Grimace (reflex irritability), Activity (muscle tone), and Respiration — scored 0-2 each, total 0-10
questions.explanation: APGAR score: rapid assessment of newborn at 1 and 5 minutes after birth. A: Appearance (skin color — blue/pale=0, acrocyanosis=1, pink=2). P: Pulse (heart rate — absent=0, <100=1, >100=2). G: Grimace (reflex irritability — none=0, grimace=1, cry=2). A: Activity (muscle tone — limp=0, some flexion=1, active=2). R: Respiration (effort — absent=0, slow/irregular=1, good cry=2). Score: 7-10: normal. 4-6: moderate depression. 0-3: severe (needs resuscitation). 1-min: need for resuscitation. 5-min: response to resuscitation. Reference: OP Ghai — APGAR.

questions.questionText: Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) in neonates most commonly affects:
questions.optionB: Premature/low birth weight infants (most common GI emergency in neonates — ischemic bowel necrosis with pneumatosis intestinalis on XR)
questions.explanation: NEC: most common GI emergency in neonates. Primarily affects premature/LBW infants (inversely proportional to gestational age). Pathogenesis: intestinal immaturity + enteral feeding + bacterial colonization → mucosal ischemia → necrosis. Presentation: feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, bilious vomiting, systemic sepsis. Pathognomonic XR: pneumatosis intestinalis (air in bowel wall). Portal venous gas (advanced). Pneumoperitoneum: perforation (surgical emergency). Treatment: NPO, NGT, antibiotics, supportive. Surgery if perforation/failure to improve. Breast milk: protective. Reference: OP Ghai — NEC.
